extracts.extract: Develop human sources. Contact people, and talk to them. Be skeptical when something looks, sounds or seems too good to be true. Consult multiple, credible sources. Familiarize yourself with search and research methods, and new tools. Communicate and work together with other professionals — verification is a team sport.
extracts.chapter: UGC and open source information in investigations
extracts.notes: Six core principles carried from original Verification Handbook into investigative context

extracts.extract: verification is a mix of three elements: Sources knowledge, reliability and honesty, and the number, variety and reliability of sources you can find and persuade to talk. Documentation.
extracts.chapter: UGC and open source information in investigations
extracts.notes: Steve Buttry's three-element model; this Handbook focuses on the third element

extracts.extract: just as we know that human memory is faulty and that sources lie, we must also remember that documents and data aren't always what they appear.
extracts.chapter: UGC and open source information in investigations

extracts.extract: Channel 4 investigators took that previous Twitter handle and searched other social networks to see if they could find anyone using it. That led them to a Google+ account, and then to a Facebook page. There they found photos and other details about a man living in Bangalore who worked as a marketing executive for an Indian company.
extracts.chapter: UGC and open source information in investigations
extracts.notes: ShamiWitness case study: tracing a username across platforms to unmask identity

extracts.extract: The simple two-letter word 'is' reveals opinions and facts about your subject. To avoid clutter, include the company name of the person or any other detail you know, and tell Google that both words should not be that far from each other. The AROUND() operator MUST BE IN CAPITALS. It sets the maximum distance in words between the two terms.
extracts.chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
extracts.notes: Henk van Ess technique for building background profiles using AROUND() proximity operator

extracts.extract: This search is asking Google to 'Show me PDF documents with the name of the CEO of Shell in it, but exclude documents from Shell.' This will find documents about your subject, but not from the company of the subject itself. This helps you to see what opponents, competitors or opinionated people say about your subject.
extracts.chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
extracts.notes: filetype:pdf combined with -site: to surface third-party perspectives on a subject

extracts.extract: Search for worldwide official documents about this person. It searches for gov.uk (United Kingdom) but also .gov.au (Australia), .gov.cn (China), .gov (U.S.) and other governmental websites in the world. If you don't have a .gov website in your country, use the local word for it with the site: operator.
extracts.chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
extracts.notes: Using site:gov.* variants to find official documents across jurisdictions

extracts.extract: With this formula you can find results that use different spellings of the name. You will receive documents with the word Shell, but not those that include 'Ben' as the first name. With this, you will find out that he is also referred to as Bernardus van Beurden. Now repeat steps a, b, c and d with this new name.
extracts.chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
extracts.notes: Iterative technique: discover name variants then re-run all previous search strategies with new spellings

extracts.extract: Most of the research you do is not based on today, but an earlier period. Always tell your search engine to go back in time.
extracts.chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
extracts.notes: Using date-restricted image/web search to find pre-event documentation

extracts.extract: Websites often cease to exist. There is a chance you can still view them by using archive.org. This tool can do its work only if you know the URL of the webpage you want to see. The problem is that often the page is gone and therefore you don't know it. So how do you find a seemingly disappeared URL? Step One: Find an index. Step Two: Put the index in the time machine. Step Three: Find the original website.
extracts.chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
extracts.notes: Three-step method: find an index page that linked to the dead URL, look up that index in archive.org, extract the dead URL, then retrieve it

extracts.extract: Search engines like Google don't actually know what web pages are about. They do, however, know the words that are on the pages. So to get a search engine to behave itself, you need to work out which words are on your target pages. Choose your search terms wisely. Each word you add to the search focuses the results by eliminating results that don't include your chosen keywords. Some words are on every page you are after. Other words might or might not be on the target page. Avoid those subjective keywords, as they can eliminate useful pages from the results.
extracts.notes: Core principle: think about what words will appear on the target page, not what the page is about

extracts.extract: If you don't have definite keywords, you can still build in other possible keywords without damaging the results. Pages discussing heroin use in Texas might not include the word 'Texas'; they may just mention the names of different cities. You can build these into your search as optional keywords by separating them with the word OR (in capital letters).
extracts.notes: Using OR for optional keywords when target pages may use synonyms or specifics instead of general terms

extracts.extract: You can focus your search on a particular site by using the search syntax 'site:' followed by the domain name. You can also use it to focus your search on municipal and academic sources, too. This is particularly effective when researching countries that use unique domain types for government and university sites.

Some information comes in certain types of file formats. For instance, statistics, figures and data often appear in Excel spreadsheets. Professionally produced reports can often be found in PDF documents. You can specify a format in your search by using 'filetype:' followed by the desired data file extension.
extracts.notes: Matching expected file format to data type: spreadsheets for stats, PDFs for reports

extracts.extract: Start by building a dossier on the person you're trying to locate or learn more about. This can include: The person's name, bearing in mind different variations, the spelling of foreign names in Roman letters, whether names changed when a person married, middle names or initials. The town the person lives in or was born in. The person's job and company. The person's phone number. Any of the person's usernames, as these are often constant across various social networks. The person's email address. A photograph, as this can help you find the right person if the name is common.
extracts.notes: Comprehensive checklist of identifiers to collect before beginning a person search

extracts.extract: People often use the same photo as a profile picture for different social networks. This being the case, reverse image search on sites like TinEye and Google Images, will help you identify linked accounts.

Many journalists have been fooled by malicious websites. Since it's easy for anyone to buy an unclaimed .com, .net or .org site, we should not go on face value. A site that looks well produced and has an authentic-sounding domain name may still be a political hoax, false company or satirical prank. Some degree of quality control can be achieved by examining the domain name itself. Google it and visit any sites that offer the ability to perform a whois search. It will bring up the registration details given by the site owner when the domain name was purchased.

Whois is not a panacea for verification. People can often get away with lying on a domain registration form. Some people will use an anonymizing service like Domains by Proxy, but combining a whois search with other domain name and IP address tools forms a valuable weapon in the battle to provide useful material from authentic sources.
extracts.notes: Caveat: WHOIS alone is insufficient; must combine with other checks

extracts.extract: Everything has a paper trail, a lead that exposes the systemic underwire of a network, company, or person's illicit or illegal activities. The trick is to find it.
extracts.chapter: Records and publicly available material to investigate companies

extracts.extract: The most important aspects of any investigation are to dig, listen and ask pertinent questions. But asking questions requires context, and listening to the right sources means finding the core of the story. Data, free or otherwise, can never replace good investigative research. In order to do good investigative research, these days, one must become familiar with how and where knowledge can be found, and how best to access and develop it.
extracts.notes: Data supplements but does not replace investigative research

extracts.extract: We gathered corporate brochures that listed financial receiving agents, brokers, auditors, physical offices and other details that detailed connections between seemingly independent companies.
extracts.notes: Using corporate promotional material as a source for mapping shell-company networks

extracts.extract: Our work made use of free public databases such as Duedil that allow for individual and corporate director searches. These enable users to identify the number of companies — current, dissolved, etc. — a director is involved in. It can also provide other important information: Shareholders, registered offices and a timeline of retired and current individuals involved. We also used LinkedIn to probe personal and corporate connections.
extracts.notes: Free tools for mapping corporate networks across jurisdictions

extracts.extract: Along with databases, we used Whois Internet searches where possible to determine the date of creation and ownership information of websites that were connected to the network. We then cross-referenced the contact details of the websites with the information listed in corporate databases for brokers and shell entities. Using specific search phrases, we were able to draw out mentions of certain names, companies, products etc. from various files on the Internet.
extracts.notes: Cross-referencing WHOIS data with corporate registries to confirm network links

extracts.extract: As part of the investigation, we also created dummy profiles on social media to enable us to connect with relevant companies and individuals, and to engage in email communication. We posed as potential investors to gain firsthand access to the push and pull of the scam.
extracts.notes: Ethical grey area: creating fake identities to gather evidence

extracts.extract: Unlike other jurisdictions, African countries often do not have centralized or electronically accessible data. To this end, we train and deploy in-country researchers to physically obtain not just the updated and accurate corporate, land, court and other data, but also to visit critical locations, conduct basic interviews and take relevant photos.
extracts.notes: Organizational solution to the problem of non-digitized records in some jurisdictions

extracts.extract: We connected with Heinrich Böhmke, a South African prosecutor and an in-house expert at ANCIR, to 'cross-examine' our evidence. This is a process Böhmke took from the legal world and adapted for investigative journalism. We looked for bias, contradictions, consistency and probability within evidence, resources, interviews and sources.
extracts.notes: Adapting legal cross-examination technique to stress-test journalistic evidence

extracts.extract: By identifying the broad relations within, and between, people, companies, jurisdictions and numbers, we were able to visualize the network's structure. This told us how the scheme functioned and who was involved.

It doesn't take a genius to develop a good investigation or to lift the corporate veil — it simply takes curiosity, technique and a commitment to read as much and as far into the issue as possible. Scour as many data sources as possible: Corporate, media, NGO, shipping, sanctions, courts… Look for what is not obvious, seems illogical, or that just plain sticks out to you. Follow your instinct. Ask as many questions as possible.
extracts.notes: Followed by a checklist of questions to ask about corporate entities

extracts.extract: The most important lesson I've learned from two decades of using data for investigations is that data lies just as much as people, or even more so. Data, after all, is often created and maintained by people. Data is meant to be a representation of the reality of a particular moment of time. So, how do we verify that a data set corresponds to reality?
extracts.notes: Giannina Segnini's core axiom from two decades of data investigation

extracts.extract: Two key verification tasks need to be performed during a data-driven investigation: An initial evaluation must occur immediately after getting the data; and findings must be verified at the end of the investigation or analysis phase.
extracts.notes: Two-checkpoint model: verify on receipt and verify after analysis

extracts.extract: The first practice I recommend is to explore the extreme values (highest or lowest) for each variable in a dataset, and to then count how many records (rows) are listed within each of the possible values.
extracts.notes: First step of initial verification: check extremes and value distributions

extracts.extract: If we create a pivot table to count how many projects have a zero cost, in relation to the total records, we can see how more than half of those (53 percent) cost zero. This means that anyone who performs a calculation or analysis per country, region or year involving the cost of the projects would be wrong if they failed to account for all of the entries with no stated cost. Using the dataset as it's provided will lead to an inaccurate conclusion.
extracts.notes: World Bank example: 53% zero-cost entries would corrupt any cost-based analysis

extracts.extract: A common mistake made when working with data is to fail to identify the existence of duplicate records. Whenever processing disaggregated data or information about people, companies, events or transactions, the first step is to search for a unique identification variable for each item.

One of the best ways to assess a dataset's credibility is to choose a sample record and compare it against reality. It is always recommended to repeat this validation exercise on a significant sample of the records.
extracts.notes: Spot-check sample records against original documentation or real-world conditions

extracts.extract: From the moment it's first entered in a computer to the time when we access it, data goes through several input, storage, transmission and registry processes. At any stage, it may be manipulated by people and information systems. It is therefore very common that relations between tables or fields get lost or mixed up, or that some variables fail to get updated. This is why it's essential to perform integrity tests.
extracts.notes: Data degrades through its lifecycle; integrity tests check that relationships between fields remain valid

extracts.extract: Open Refine is an excellent tool to quickly explore and evaluate the quality of databases. You can use Open Refine to run a numeric facet in the Amount field. A numeric facet groups numbers into numeric range bins. This enables you to select any range that spans a consecutive number of bins. You can generate a histogram with the values range included in the database. Records can then be filtered by values by moving the arrows inside the graph. The same can be done for dates and text values.

It is therefore mandatory to learn how to decrypt codes and to understand how they are used to better understand the logic behind databases and, more importantly, their relations. Each one of the 17 million cargo containers in the world has a unique identifier, and we can track them if we understand that the first four letters of the identifier are related to the identity of its owner.
extracts.notes: Decoding classification systems is both a data-quality practice and a story-finding technique

extracts.extract: This example shows how decoding acronyms is not only a best practice for evaluating the quality of data, but, more important, to finding stories of public interest.
extracts.notes: World Bank structural adjustment codes contradicted official policy claims — decoding revealed discrepancy

extracts.extract: Before the analysis, we made sure to evaluate and clean all of the records, and to verify the relationships between each person and their assets.
extracts.notes: La Nacion Avancemos subsidy investigation

extracts.extract: It was not until we went to visit their homes that we could prove what the data alone could have never told us: These kids lived in real poverty with their mothers because they had been abandoned by their fathers. Not even the best data analysis can replace on-the-ground journalism and field verification.
extracts.notes: Core lesson: data analysis must be validated by real-world reporting; the data showed fathers with assets but the reality was abandonment

extracts.extract: I compared the position of the minaret, the dome and a nearby wall on the satellite map imagery to the video, and it was clear it was a perfect match. Now that the likely position of the camera in the town was established, I could watch the whole video comparing other details to what was visible on satellite map imagery. This further confirmed the locations matched.
extracts.notes: Higgins' Tiji video: matching mosque minaret/dome position between video and satellite imagery

extracts.extract: As they walked through the streets, it was possible to map out the roads along the route they took, and then match that pattern to what was visible in satellite map imagery.
extracts.notes: When no large landmarks exist, map the route pattern from video and match to satellite imagery

extracts.extract: While things like billboards and streetlights are small objects, the shadows they cast can actually indicate their presence. Shadows can also be used to reveal information about the comparative height of buildings, and the shape of those buildings. I used the website Sun Calc, which allows users to calculate the position of the sun throughout the day using a Google Maps based interface. It was then possible to establish the time of day as approximately 12:30 p.m. local time.
extracts.notes: Shadows reveal both presence of small objects and time of day via sun position calculation

extracts.extract: By bringing together different sources, tools and techniques, it was possible to connect these individual pieces of information and establish critical facts about this incident.
extracts.notes: MH17 Buk missile investigation: combining geolocation, chronolocation, social media, interviews

extracts.extract: A key element of working with user-generated content in investigations is understanding how that content is shared. With Syria, a handful of opposition social media pages are the main sources of information from certain areas. This obviously limits the perspective on the conflict from different regions, but also means it's possible to collect, organize and systematically review those accounts for the latest information.
extracts.notes: Understanding the distribution architecture of UGC is prerequisite to systematic collection

extracts.extract: The videos were uncovered by first geolocating the initial videos we found, then using that to predict the likely route those vehicles would have taken to get from each geolocated site. Then we would keyword search on various social media sites for the names of locations that were along the route the vehicle would have had to travel. We also searched for keywords such as 'convoy,' 'missile,' etc. that could be associated with sightings.
extracts.notes: Iterative method: geolocate > predict route > keyword search along route > geolocate new finds

extracts.extract: If there's one final piece of advice, it would be to give this work and approach a try in any investigation. It's remarkable what you can turn up when you approach UGC and open source information in a systematic way. You tend to learn quickly by just doing it. Even something as simple as double-checking the geolocation someone else has done can teach you a lot about comparing videos and photographs to satellite map imagery.

In a human rights investigation, we compare all facts gathered with relevant human rights norms and laws to make determinations of violations or abuses. Consequently, a single analyst who looks at UGC, such as myself, must be part of a team comprising relevant country, policy and legal experts.
extracts.notes: UGC analyst must work within team of country, policy, and legal specialists

extracts.extract: Today we are achieving the best results when combining a variety of evidence, such as testimony, official documents, satellite imagery, and UGC. This requires the close collaboration of researchers who possess country expertise, trusted contacts on the ground, and highly specialized analysts who do not focus on a specific region or country, but are able to provide analysis based on satellite imagery or UGC.
extracts.notes: Best results from combining testimony + documents + satellite + UGC across specialist roles

extracts.extract: In some instances, one piece of evidence does not corroborate some of the information gathered during the investigation, such as when satellite imagery does not support eyewitness claims of a large mass grave. We then exercise caution and hold back on making statements of fact or determinations of violations.
extracts.notes: When evidence types contradict, withhold conclusions rather than privilege one source

extracts.extract: We first performed standard content analysis. This involved extracting the specifications of the roads, street lamps, buildings and vegetation, as well as details related to the people seen in the video such as clothes and military equipment. Reviewing the video frame by frame greatly aided with this process. The geographic features were then compared to satellite images of the area on Google Earth.
extracts.notes: Amnesty International's standard procedure for video verification: extract visual features then match to satellite imagery

extracts.extract: Several photographs in our possession were actually geotagged. We discovered this by using a EXIF viewer to examine the metadata in the photo. This location data proved a perfect match to the street corner we identified as part of the content analysis of the initial video.
extracts.notes: EXIF geotag data corroborated the location established through video content analysis

extracts.extract: We followed the video to perform a content analysis in order to identify distinctive markings on the soldiers and victims, or anything that could indicate location, time or date. This revealed two important details: a soldier wearing a black flak jacket stating 'Borno State. Operation Flush,' the name of the military operation in northeastern Nigeria; and, for a split second, an ID number on a rifle became visible.
extracts.notes: Frame-by-frame review to extract unit identifiers and operational markings from video

extracts.extract: An AI researcher was also able to secure actual video files while on a field mission to the area. This allowed us to conduct metadata analysis. The metadata corroborated that the footage had been created March 14, 2014. Obtaining the original files had been possible only through well-established local contacts and networks, who might share content in person or via email (ideally encrypted).
extracts.notes: Original files preserve metadata stripped during upload; requires trusted local contacts

extracts.extract: Savvy news desk researchers and journalists who might be inclined to contact local sources via Twitter or other public platform should consider the risk implications for asking for such sensitive footage from contacts in insecure environments.
extracts.notes: Warning against public-platform contact for sensitive material in conflict zones

extracts.extract: It's essential to determine what ethical standards will work for you and your audience, and what actions will allow you to establish and preserve a relationship with them. Our approach must be ethical so that it can be sustainable.
extracts.notes: Ethical UGC practices framed as prerequisite for sustainable source relationships

extracts.extract: The key takeaway here is to consider how the content creator sees their activity, rather than how the platform defines it. The main issue is likely to be how you identify yourself to and within that community.
extracts.notes: Creator's perception of privacy matters more than platform's technical settings

extracts.extract: Seeking permission to use content from creators of UGC helps establish and maintain the reputation of your organization as one that gives fair treatment. Securing permission will also help you ensure you are using content from an original source. This means the act of asking for permission also opens up a potential source of additional information on the content that you otherwise wouldn't have had.
extracts.notes: Permission request doubles as verification step and source-development opportunity

extracts.extract: Does an assignment put someone at risk? Could an individual get too close to a dangerous event or to people who may cause them harm? What is your responsibility to a person who is harmed while carrying out an assignment set by you? How will you identify this person in the publication or broadcast? What impact does an assignment have on the honesty/authenticity of the content being produced versus something that was created unprompted?
extracts.notes: Five questions to establish policy before soliciting audience contributions

extracts.extract: When someone uploads a photograph or video to a social network, the copyright remains with them. So if you want to download the picture or video to use elsewhere, you must first seek permission. If you simply want to embed the material, using the embed code provided by all of the social networks, legally you don't need to seek permission. Ethically, however, it might be appropriate to contact the person who created the content to let them know how and where you intend to use it.
extracts.notes: Legal distinction between embedding (no permission needed) and downloading (permission required)

extracts.extract: An important question to ask is whether the person actually captured the material him or herself. It is amazing how many people upload other people's content on their own channels. They will often 'give permission' for use even though they have no right to do so. You also want to ask basic questions about the location, and what else they could see, to help you authenticate what they claim to have witnessed.
extracts.notes: Re-uploaders frequently grant permission they don't own; always confirm original capture

extracts.extract: If you do seek permission on the social network itself, make sure that you take a screenshot of the exchange. People will sometimes provide permission for use, and then, after negotiating an exclusive deal with another organization, they will delete any exchanges on social media to avoid showing them giving permission to others.

Best practice is to label any content with the information you can confirm, whether that's source, date or location. If you can confirm only two of three, add this information over the photo or video. The most important role for journalists is to provide the necessary context about the content that is being shared: debunk what is false, and provide crucial information about time, date or location, as well as showing how this content relates to other material.
extracts.notes: Alternative to 'cannot be independently verified' caveat: label what you CAN confirm

extracts.extract: We must seek informed consent, not just consent, meaning: Does the uploader understand what they are giving permission for? And when it comes to crediting, you must talk to them about whether and how they would like credit.

Be aware that embedded content will disappear from your site if it is removed from the social network by the original uploader. So you should ultimately try to procure the original file, especially if you are planning to run the content for a long time.

It is best practice to 'label' who has captured the content. Was it a UNHCR staff member? Was it a freelance journalist? Was it a citizen journalist? Was it a refugee? When someone unrelated to the newsroom takes a picture that is used by the newsroom, for reasons of transparency, any affiliation should be explained to the audience. Simply labeling this type of material as 'Amateur Footage' or something similar doesn't provide the necessary context.
extracts.notes: Specificity in labeling: identify the creator's role/affiliation, not generic 'amateur' label

extracts.extract: Spiegel has very specific and detailed guidelines for fact-checking. This process ensures we apply the same standard to all work, and helps ensure we do not overlook key facts or aspects of a story. Any fact that is to be published will be checked to see if it is correct on its own and in context. Every verifiable piece of information will be underlined. Standardized marks will be used to denote statements as correct, incorrect, not verifiable, etc. Correct facts and figures will be checked off. If corrections are necessary, they will be noted. The source of factual corrections and quotations must be given.
extracts.notes: Der Spiegel's documentation department: 70 specialist fact-checkers using standardized manuscript markings

extracts.extract: When fact-checking a manuscript, other and if possible more accurate sources than the author's sources should be used. A statement is considered verified only if confirmed by reliable sources or experts. If a piece of research contradicts an author's statement, the author must be notified of the contradiction during the discussion of the manuscript. If a fact is unverifiable, the author must also be notified.
extracts.notes: Fact-checkers must use sources independent of the author's; unverifiable facts flagged to author

extracts.extract: Sometimes the limited time available means that priorities must be set. In such cases, facts that are the clear responsibility of the fact-checker must be checked first, particularly: Are the times and dates correct? Does the text contradict itself? Are the names and offices/jobs correct? Are the quotations correct (in wording and in context)? How current and trustworthy are the sources used?
extracts.notes: Five-item priority checklist when time is limited: dates, internal consistency, names/titles, quotations, source currency

extracts.extract: Fact-checking starts with comparing a story draft with the research materials provided by the author. The fact-checker then seeks to verify the facts and assertions by gathering additional sources that are independent of each other. For crucial passages, the checker examines a wide variety of sources in order to examine what is commonly accepted and believed and what is a more subjective or biased point of view. They determine what is a matter of fact and what is controversial or, in some cases, a myth.
extracts.notes: Three-layer process: compare to author sources, gather independent sources, distinguish consensus from bias

extracts.extract: Prefer original documents. If an academic study is quoted, obtain the original, full text. If company earnings are cited, obtain their financials. Do not rely on press summaries and press releases when the original document can be obtained. Prefer sources that delineate between facts and opinion, and that supply facts in their work. Prefer sources that clearly indicate the source of their information, as this enables you to verify their work. Beware of sources that make factual errors about basic facts, or that confuse basic concepts about a subject matter.
extracts.notes: Four guidelines for evaluating sources: prefer originals, prefer fact/opinion distinction, prefer cited sources, beware basic errors

extracts.extract: Accuracy is the basic prerequisite for good journalism and objective reportage. Journalists make mistakes, intended or not. Mistakes damage the most valuable asset of journalism: credibility. The method to reduce the probability of mistakes is verification; that is, checking facts before publication.
extracts.notes: Supported by data: 1,153 corrections found in a single Spiegel issue by Hamburg University thesis

extracts.extract: In a frame-by-frame analysis of roughly three hours of an archived livestream of the protest (324,000 frames!) the defense team uncovered one frame of video that showed that the police vehicle Seixas was being accused of having set ablaze was the exact same vehicle that drove him away after he was detained. This was proven by comparing identifying characteristics of the vehicle in the video with the one that Seixas was transported in.
extracts.chapter: Video to help prove innocence of an activist in Rio
extracts.notes: WITNESS case: exhaustive frame-by-frame review of 324K frames to find exculpatory evidence

extracts.extract: WITNESS helped the defense identify and prepare this evidence, both by assembling screenshots of these videos into a storyboard as well as by editing a 10-minute evidentiary submission video that was delivered to the judge, along with the accompanying documentation.
extracts.chapter: Video to help prove innocence of an activist in Rio
extracts.notes: Packaging video evidence for legal use: storyboard of screenshots + edited submission video + documentation

extracts.extract: Reymond took a full two hours to verify its authenticity before posting it to his Twitter account. Reymond's process of verification included comparing the facial features of the man in the photo to the mugshot that later emerged, and cross-referencing other details that emerged as witnesses and officials shared details of the attack.
extracts.notes: Two-hour verification before publishing: facial comparison + cross-referencing witness details

extracts.extract: The claim that the photo of Zehaf-Bibeau originated with an ISIS account is categorically false. The ISIS account that circulated the photo acquired it hours after it was originally posted to Twitter. The temptation to draw a connection between vivid photos like this one and our worst fears is enormous. Avoiding this temptation is one of the chief responsibilities of 21st century journalists.
extracts.notes: Ottawa shooting case: journalists attributed photo to ISIS when it actually came from a journalist; technical metadata proved provenance

extracts.extract: The first step is to determine which language to search for the information you need, and then to find the spelling in Arabic. However, keep in mind that the pronunciation of a single word can differ greatly among Arabic-speaking countries. Be aware that even within the same registry you should search using multiple spellings of the same word.
extracts.chapter: Searching for companies in the Middle East
extracts.notes: Company names may have multiple valid Arabic transliterations across countries; must search all variants

extracts.extract: These examples demonstrate how an understanding of cultures, languages and other factors can play a role in ensuring how effectively you can make use of public data and information during an investigation.
extracts.chapter: Searching for companies in the Middle East
